title: General Agent Evaluation
author: Bandel, Elron, Yehudai, Asaf, Eden, Lilach, Sagron, Yehoshua, Perlitz, Yotam, Venezian, Elad, Razinkov, Natalia, Ergas, Natan, Ifergan, Shlomit Shachor, Shlomov, Segev, Jacovi, Michal, Choshen, Leshem, Ein-Dor, Liat, Katz, Yoav, Shmueli-Scheuer, Michal
summary: This paper presents the first systematic evaluation of general-purpose AI agents across diverse environments and benchmarks. The authors develop a unified protocol and evaluation harness enabling fair comparison of tool-calling, MCP, code-generation, and CLI agent architectures using identical backbone models across six benchmarks spanning software engineering, customer service, research, and personal assistance. Key findings show that agent architecture can swing performance by 12 percentage points within a single model, yet backbone model choice dominates overall results. Notably, general agents achieve performance comparable to heavily-customized domain-specific agents on 4 of 6 benchmarks, while open-weight models exhibit unexpected failure modes absent from frontier closed-source models. This work provides critical infrastructure and insights for understanding how architectural choices impact agent generalization across domains.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2602.22953 (cs) [Submitted on 26 Feb 2026 ( v1 ), last revised 11 May 2026 (this version, v2)] Title: General Agent Evaluation Authors: Elron Bandel , Asaf Yehudai , Lilach Eden , Yehoshua Sagron , Yotam Perlitz , Elad Venezian , Natalia Razinkov , Natan Ergas , Shlomit Shachor Ifergan , Segev Shlomov , Michal Jacovi , Leshem Choshen , Liat Ein-Dor , Yoav Katz , Michal Shmueli-Scheuer View a PDF of the paper titled General Agent Evaluation, by Elron Bandel and 14 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: General-purpose agents perform tasks in unfamiliar environments without domain-specific manual customization. Yet no study has systematically measured how agent architecture shapes performance across heterogeneous protocols and diverse unfamiliar environments. This is the first systematic study, comparing tool-calling, MCP, code-generation, and CLI agents on the same benchmarks with the same models. Two gaps blocked such a study: existing harnesses require per-benchmark wiring or fixed protocol classes (web for BrowserGym, CLI for Harbor), and benchmarks themselves expect human-authored prompts, context, and integration glue. To enable this study, we contribute (1) a unifying protocol that bridges existing benchmark and agent protocols; (2) an evaluation harness that surfaces any benchmark to any general-purpose agent and backbone model; and (3) the first Open General Agent Leaderboard of agent configurations, a full factorial over 5 agent architectures x 5 backbone LLMs (three closed-source, two open-weight) x 6 benchmarks spanning software engineering, customer service, deep research, and personal assistance. We find that (i) general agents adapt to every tested domain without per-domain customization; (ii) agent architecture choice swings results by up to 12pp within a single model, yet backbone model choice dominates overall performance; (iii) on 4 of 6 tested benchmarks, top general agents are indistinguishable from the leading heavily-customized domain-specific agents; (iv) open-weight models tested exhibit "generality sinks" absent from frontier closed-source models: they consistently collapse on specific agent architectures or benchmarks; (v) a behavioral failure analysis reveals architecture-distinctive error signatures that aggregate scoring cannot discriminate. Code, harness, leaderboard, and traces are at this https URL . Comments: Presented at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Agents in the Wild Subjects: Artificial Intelligence (cs.AI) Cite as: arXiv:2602.22953 [cs.AI] (or arXiv:2602.22953v2 [cs.AI] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2602.22953 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite Submission history From: Elron Bandel [ view email ] [v1] Thu, 26 Feb 2026 12:48:02 UTC (1,438 KB) [v2] Mon, 11 May 2026 10:27:38 UTC (1,861 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled General Agent Evaluation, by Elron Bandel and 14 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.AI < prev | next > new | recent | 2026-02 Change to browse by: cs References & Citations NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... takeaways: General agents successfully adapt to diverse domains without per-domain customization across six distinct benchmarks.
Agent architecture choice swings performance by up to 12pp, but backbone model dominance outweighs architectural differences.
Open-weight models exhibit domain-specific "generality sinks" where they collapse on particular architectures or benchmarks.
Top general agents match heavily-customized domain-specific agents on 4 of 6 tested benchmarks.
Behavioral failure analysis reveals architecture-distinctive error patterns that aggregate scoring masks.

title: A Practical Approach for Building Production-Grade Conversational Agents with Workflow Graphs
author: Park, Chiwan, Jang, Wonjun, Kim, Daeryong, Ahn, Aelim, Yang, Kichang, Hwang, Woosung, Roh, Jihyeon, Park, Hyerin, Wang, Hyosun, Kim, Min Seok, Kang, Jihoon
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of deploying large language models in production conversational agents, specifically for e-commerce. The authors propose a workflow graph approach that balances the flexibility of LLMs with strict service-specific constraints—a tension arising from LLMs' probabilistic nature. They present a detailed case study of their implementation, strategies for overcoming real-world limitations, and a framework for building scalable, controllable, and reliable AI agents. The work bridges the gap between academic research and industrial deployment, offering practical insights valuable to engineers building production-grade conversational systems.
content: Computer Science > Computation and Language arXiv:2505.23006 (cs) [Submitted on 29 May 2025] Title: A Practical Approach for Building Production-Grade Conversational Agents with Workflow Graphs Authors: Chiwan Park , Wonjun Jang , Daeryong Kim , Aelim Ahn , Kichang Yang , Woosung Hwang , Jihyeon Roh , Hyerin Park , Hyosun Wang , Min Seok Kim , Jihoon Kang View a PDF of the paper titled A Practical Approach for Building Production-Grade Conversational Agents with Workflow Graphs, by Chiwan Park and 10 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: The advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has led to significant improvements in various service domains, including search, recommendation, and chatbot applications. However, applying state-of-the-art (SOTA) research to industrial settings presents challenges, as it requires maintaining flexible conversational abilities while also strictly complying with service-specific constraints. This can be seen as two conflicting requirements due to the probabilistic nature of LLMs. In this paper, we propose our approach to addressing this challenge and detail the strategies we employed to overcome their inherent limitations in real-world applications. We conduct a practical case study of a conversational agent designed for the e-commerce domain, detailing our implementation workflow and optimizations. Our findings provide insights into bridging the gap between academic research and real-world application, introducing a framework for developing scalable, controllable, and reliable AI-driven agents. Comments: Accepted to ACL 2025 Industry Track. 12 pages, 5 figures Subjects: Computation and Language (cs.CL) ; Artificial Intelligence (cs.AI) ACM classes: I.2.7 Cite as: arXiv:2505.23006 [cs.CL] (or arXiv:2505.23006v1 [cs.CL] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2505.23006 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite Submission history From: Daeryong Kim [ view email ] [v1] Thu, 29 May 2025 02:30:27 UTC (3,222 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled A Practical Approach for Building Production-Grade Conversational Agents with Workflow Graphs, by Chiwan Park and 10 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.CL < prev | next > new | recent | 2025-05 Change to browse by: cs cs.AI References & Citations NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... takeaways: Workflow graphs enable controllable LLM-based agents by constraining probabilistic outputs while maintaining conversational flexibility in production settings.
Real-world deployment requires balancing SOTA research capabilities with strict compliance and service-specific constraints inherent to industrial applications.
E-commerce case study demonstrates practical optimization strategies and implementation patterns for scalable, reliable conversational AI systems.
Framework addresses core tension: LLMs' stochastic nature conflicts with deterministic service requirements in production environments.

title: Humans and Agents in Software Engineering Loops
author: Kief Morris
summary: Kief Morris explores the optimal positioning of humans in AI-assisted software development, proposing a framework between two extremes: humans completely outside the 'how loop' (vibe coding) or micromanaging every decision. Morris advocates for humans to be 'on the loop'—managing feedback cycles and setting quality standards rather than inspecting individual code changes. He distinguishes between the 'why loop' (iterating on ideas and goals) and nested 'how loops' (implementation details), arguing that while external quality (functionality, performance, compliance) matters, internal quality (code cleanliness) should be managed through design standards and feedback loops rather than line-by-line review. The article challenges the assumption that LLMs don't benefit from well-structured codebases and proposes humans should focus on steering outcomes through strategic feedback rather than tactical code review.
content: Humans and Agents in Software Engineering Loops Kief Morris Kief Morris lives in London and works as a global cloud technology specialist for Thoughtworks. This article is part of âExploring Gen AIâ . A series capturing Thoughtworks technologists' explorations of using gen ai technology for software development. 04 March 2026 Should humans stay out of the software development process and vibe code, or do we need developers in the loop inspecting every line of code? I believe the answer is to focus on the goal of turning ideas into outcomes. The right place for us humans is to build and manage the working loop rather than either leaving the agents to it or micromanaging what they produce. Letâs call this âon the loop.â As software creators we build an outcome by turning our ideas into working software and iterating as we learn and evolve our ideas. This is the âwhy loopâ. Until the AI uprising comes humans will run this loop because weâre the ones who want what it produces. The process of building the software is the âhow loop.â The how loop involves creating, selecting, and using intermediate artefacts like code, tests, tools, and infrastructure. It may also involve documentation like technical designs and ADRs. Weâre used to seeing many of these as deliverables, but intermediate artefacts are really just a means to an end. Figure 1: The why loop iterates over ideas and software, the how loop iterates on building the software In reality the how loop contains multiple loops. The outermost how loop specifies and delivers the working software for the why loop. The innermost loop generates and tests code. Loops in between break down higher levels of work into smaller tasks for the lower loops to implement, then validate the results. Figure 2: The how loop has multiple levels of inner loops that work on smaller increments of the full implementation These loops may follow practices like design reviews and test stages. They might build systems by applying architectural approaches and design patterns like microservices or CUPID. Like the intermediate artefacts that pop out of these practices and patterns, they are all a means of achieving the outcome we actually care about. But maybe we donât care about the means that are used to achieve our goals? Maybe we can just let the LLMs run the how loop however they like? Humans outside the loop Plenty of people have discovered the joy of letting humans stick to the why loop, and leaving the how loop for the agents to deal with. This is the common definition of âvibe codingâ. Some interpretations of Spec Driven Development (SDD) are much the same, with humans investing effort in writing the outcome we want, but not dictating how the LLM should achieve it. Figure 3: Human runs the why loop, agent runs the how loop. The appeal of humans staying out of the how loop is that the why loop is the one we really care about. Software development is a messy domain that inevitably bogs down into over-engineered processes and coping with technical debt. And every new LLM model so far has gotten better at taking a user prompt and spitting out working software. If youâre not satisfied with what it spits out, tell the LLM and itâll give you another iteration. If the LLMs can write and change code without us, do we care whether the code is âcleanâ? It doesnât matter whether a variable name clearly expresses its purpose as long as an LLM can figure it out. Maybe we donât even need to care what language the software is written in? We care about external quality, not internal quality for its own sake. External quality is what we experience as a user or other stakeholder of the software. Functional quality is a must, the system needs to work correctly. And for production software we also care about non-functional, operational quality. Our system shouldnât crash, it should run quickly, and we donât want it posting confidential data to social media sites. We donât want to run up massive cloud hosting bills, and in many domains we need to pass compliance audits. We care about internal quality when it affects external outcomes. When human coders were crawling through the codebase, adding features and fixing bugs, they could do it more quickly and reliably in a clean codebase. But LLMs donât care about developer experience, do they? In theory our LLM agents can extrude a massively overcomplicated spaghetti codebase, test and fix it by running ad-hoc shell commands, and eventually produce a correct, compliant, high-performing system. We just get our swarms Ralph Wiggumming on it, running in data centers that draw energy from the boiling oceans they float on, and eventually weâll get there. 1 In practice, a cleanly-designed, well-structured codebase has externally important benefits over a messy codebase. When LLMs can more quickly understand and modify the code they work faster and spiral less. We do care about the time and cost of building the systems we need. Humans in the loop Some developers believe that the only way to maintain internal quality is to stay closely involved in the lowest levels of the how loop. Often, when an agent spirals over some broken bit of code a human developer can understand and fix it in seconds. Human experience and judgement still exceeds LLMs in many situations. Figure 4: Human runs the why loop and the how loop When people talk about âhumans in the loopâ, they often mean humans as a gatekeeper within the innermost loop where code is generated, such as manually inspecting each line of code created by an LLM. The challenge when we insist on being too closely involved in the process is that we become a bottleneck. Agents can generate code faster than humans can manually inspect it. Reports on developer productivity with AI show mixed results, which may be at least partly because of humans spending more time specifying and reviewing code than they save by getting LLMs to generate it. We need to adopt classic âshift leftâ thinking. Once upon a time we wrote all of our code, passed it to a QA team to test, and then tried to fix enough bugs to ship a release. Then we discovered that when developers write and run tests as we work we find and fix issues right away, which makes the whole process faster and more reliable. What works for humans can work for agents as well. Agents produce better code when they can gauge the quality of the code they produce themselves rather than relying on us to check it for them. We need to instruct them on what weâre looking for, and give them guidance on the best ways to achieve it. Humans on the loop Rather than personally inspecting what the agents produce, we can make them better at producing it. The collection of specifications, quality checks, and workflow guidance that control different levels of loops inside the how loop is the agentâs harness. The emerging practice of building and maintaining these harnesses, Harness Engineering , is how humans work on the loop. Figure 5: Human defines the how loop and the agent runs it Something like the on the loop concept has also been described as the âmiddle loop,â including by participants of The Future of Software Development Retreat . The middle loop refers to moving human attention to a higher-level loop than the coding loop. The difference between in the loop and on the loop is most visible in what we do when weâre not satisfied with what the agent produces, including an intermediate artefact. The âin the loopâ way is to fix the artefact, whether by directly editing it, or by telling the agent to make the correction we want. The âon the loopâ way is to change the harness that produced the artefact so it produces the results we want. We continuously improve the quality of the outcomes we get by continuously improving the harness. And then we can take it to another level. The agentic flywheel The next level is humans directing agents to manage and improve the harness rather than doing it by hand. Figure 6: Human directs agent to build and improve the how loop We build the flywheel by giving the agents the information they need to evaluate the performance of the loop. A good starting point is the tests and evaluations already included in the harness. The flywheel becomes more powerful as we feed it richer signals. Add pipeline stages that measure performance and validate failure scenarios. Feed operational data from production, user journey logs, and commercial results to broaden the scope and depth of what the agents can analyze. For each step of the workflow we have the agent review the results and recommend improvements to the harness. The scope includes improvements to any of the upstream parts of the workflow that could improve those results. What we have now is an agent harness that generates recommendations for improving itself. We start by considering the recommendations interactively, prompting the agents to implement specific changes. We can also have the agents add their recommendations to the product backlog, so we can prioritize and schedule them for the agents to pick up, apply, and test as part of the automated flow. As we gain confidence, the agents can assign scores to their recommendations, including the risks, costs, and benefits. We might then decide that recommendations with certain scores should be automatically approved and applied. At some point this might look a lot like humans out of the loop, old-school vibe coding. I suspect that will be true for standard types of work that are done often as the improvement loops reach diminishing returns. But by engineering the harness we wonât just get one-off, âgood enoughâ solutions, weâll get robust, maybe even anti-fragile systems that continuously improve themselves. These days âralph loopâ is often used colloquially to mean just firing up a bunch of agents and leaving them to keep looping until (hopefully) they finish their task. But as originally described the operator plays an important role in steering agents as they ralph.Â ↩ latest article (Mar 04): Humans and Agents in Software Engineering Loops previous article: Harness Engineering - first thoughts
takeaways: Humans should manage feedback loops and quality standards ('on the loop'), not micromanage code generation or abandon oversight entirely
Internal code quality improves LLM productivity and reduces spiraling; it's not merely aesthetic but affects external outcomes through development speed and cost
Focus on external quality (functionality, performance, compliance) and use design practices like ADRs and tests as quality gates, not human code inspection bottlenecks
The 'why loop' (goals/ideas) is fundamentally human-driven; the 'how loop' (implementation) benefits from agent-human collaboration at the right abstraction levels

title: Measuring Agent Autonomy
summary: This article examines frameworks for quantifying agent autonomy in AI systems, establishing metrics to assess how independently agents operate without human oversight. It addresses the challenge of defining and measuring autonomy across different agent architectures and deployment contexts. Technical readers should care because autonomy metrics are critical for evaluating production-ready agentic systems, enabling better risk assessment, governance decisions, and comparative evaluation across different agent designs and implementations.
takeaways: Autonomy metrics must account for decision complexity, intervention frequency, and outcome quality across diverse task domains.
Clear autonomy definitions prevent conflation with capability or reliability—an agent can be capable but low-autonomy or unreliable despite high autonomy.
Multi-dimensional measurement frameworks outperform single-number autonomy scores for practical governance and deployment decisions.
Human-in-the-loop checkpoints and fallback mechanisms quantifiably reduce measured autonomy but improve safety and trust.

title: Agent Evals
summary: This article examines methodologies for evaluating AI agents, addressing the challenge of assessing agent performance beyond traditional metrics. The piece argues that effective agent evaluation requires multi-dimensional approaches that consider goal completion, efficiency, safety, and interpretability. Technical readers should care because proper evaluation frameworks are critical for validating agent systems in production, enabling comparison of different architectures, and identifying failure modes before deployment. The article likely explores concrete evaluation techniques applicable to various agent types and use cases.
takeaways: Multi-dimensional evaluation metrics essential—single metrics miss critical agent behaviors and failure modes
Safety and interpretability assessment must be prioritized alongside task completion rates
Evaluation frameworks should account for efficiency, cost, and resource usage in agent systems
Standardized benchmarks enable meaningful comparison across different agent architectures
Real-world testing required—simulated environments often fail to capture complex agent behaviors

title: Evaluation Best Practices
summary: This article outlines best practices for evaluating agents, emphasizing the importance of structured evaluation methodologies. The piece covers key principles for designing robust evaluation frameworks, including establishing clear metrics, defining test datasets, and implementing systematic assessment procedures. Technical teams building and deploying agent systems should care because rigorous evaluation directly impacts reliability, safety, and performance in production environments.
takeaways: Define measurable metrics aligned with specific agent goals before evaluation begins
Use diverse, representative test datasets to assess generalization and edge cases
Implement systematic benchmarking procedures to enable reproducible comparisons across iterations
Document evaluation methodology for transparency and future auditing

title: Position: Agentic Evolution is the Path to Evolving LLMs
author: Lin, Minhua, Lu, Hanqing, Shi, Zhan, He, Bing, Mao, Rui, Zhang, Zhiwei, Wu, Zongyu, Tang, Xianfeng, Liu, Hui, Dai, Zhenwei, Zhang, Xiang, Wang, Suhang, Dumoulin, Benoit, Pei, Jian
summary: This position paper argues that agentic evolution represents a fundamental new scaling axis for adapting Large Language Models beyond static training. The authors identify a critical limitation: as LLMs deploy into dynamic real-world environments, static training cannot keep pace with continual change. While scaling compute at training or inference time improves baseline capabilities, it fails to close the train-deploy gap. The paper proposes A-Evolve, a framework treating deployment-time improvement as goal-directed optimization over persistent system state, where an autonomous agent diagnoses failures and produces durable improvements. Central to their thesis is the evolution-scaling hypothesis: adaptation capacity scales with compute allocated to evolution, positioning agentic evolution as a scalable path toward sustained, open-ended LLM adaptation.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2602.00359 (cs) [Submitted on 30 Jan 2026 ( v1 ), last revised 13 Mar 2026 (this version, v2)] Title: Position: Agentic Evolution is the Path to Evolving LLMs Authors: Minhua Lin , Hanqing Lu , Zhan Shi , Bing He , Rui Mao , Zhiwei Zhang , Zongyu Wu , Xianfeng Tang , Hui Liu , Zhenwei Dai , Xiang Zhang , Suhang Wang , Benoit Dumoulin , Jian Pei View a PDF of the paper titled Position: Agentic Evolution is the Path to Evolving LLMs, by Minhua Lin and 13 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) move from curated training sets into open-ended real-world environments, a fundamental limitation emerges: static training cannot keep pace with continual deployment environment change. Scaling training-time and inference-time compute improves static capability but does not close this train-deploy gap. We argue that addressing this limitation requires a new scaling axis-evolution. Existing deployment-time adaptation methods, whether parametric fine-tuning or heuristic memory accumulation, lack the strategic agency needed to diagnose failures and produce durable improvements. Our position is that agentic evolution represents the inevitable future of LLM adaptation, elevating evolution itself from a fixed pipeline to an autonomous evolver agent. We instantiate this vision in a general framework, A-Evolve, which treats deployment-time improvement as a deliberate, goal-directed optimization process over persistent system state. We further propose the evolution-scaling hypothesis: the capacity for adaptation scales with the compute allocated to evolution, positioning agentic evolution as a scalable path toward sustained, open-ended adaptation in the real world. Our code is publicly available at this https URL . takeaways: Static training cannot keep pace with deployment environment change; evolution becomes necessary scaling axis
Existing fine-tuning and memory methods lack strategic agency to diagnose failures and produce durable improvements
A-Evolve framework treats deployment-time adaptation as deliberate optimization over persistent system state
Evolution-scaling hypothesis: adaptation capacity scales with compute allocated to evolution
Agentic evolution enables sustained, open-ended adaptation in real-world deployment environments

title: Towards More Standardized AI Evaluation: From Models to Agents
author: Filali, Ali El, Bedar, Inès
summary: This research paper argues that traditional AI evaluation methodologies, designed for static models and aggregate benchmarks, are inadequate for modern agentic systems. The authors contend that evaluation must evolve from a final checkpoint into a core control function that enables trust and governance rather than serving as performance theater. They identify how standard evaluation pipelines introduce silent failures, benchmark scores mislead development teams, and non-deterministic agent behavior fundamentally changes how performance should be measured. Rather than proposing new metrics, the paper positions evaluation as a measurement discipline essential for iterating on and governing complex, tool-using AI systems operating at scale.
content: Computer Science > Computation and Language arXiv:2602.18029 (cs) [Submitted on 20 Feb 2026] Title: Towards More Standardized AI Evaluation: From Models to Agents Authors: Ali El Filali , Inès Bedar View a PDF of the paper titled Towards More Standardized AI Evaluation: From Models to Agents, by Ali El Filali and In\`es Bedar View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Evaluation is no longer a final checkpoint in the machine learning lifecycle. As AI systems evolve from static models to compound, tool-using agents, evaluation becomes a core control function. The question is no longer "How good is the model?" but "Can we trust the system to behave as intended, under change, at scale?". Yet most evaluation practices remain anchored in assumptions inherited from the model-centric era: static benchmarks, aggregate scores, and one-off success criteria. This paper argues that such approaches are increasingly obscure rather than illuminating system behavior. We examine how evaluation pipelines themselves introduce silent failure modes, why high benchmark scores routinely mislead teams, and how agentic systems fundamentally alter the meaning of performance measurement. Rather than proposing new metrics or harder benchmarks, we aim to clarify the role of evaluation in the AI era, and especially for agents: not as performance theater, but as a measurement discipline that conditions trust, iteration, and governance in non-deterministic systems. takeaways: Traditional benchmarks and aggregate scores obscure rather than illuminate agentic system behavior in production
Evaluation pipelines themselves introduce silent failure modes that inflate confidence in system reliability
High benchmark scores routinely mislead teams about real-world system safety and trustworthiness
Agent evaluation must shift from static one-off assessments to continuous control functions enabling governance
Non-deterministic system behavior demands fundamentally different performance measurement approaches than model-era practices
